title: NLP & Embeddings in Practice
pitchHtml: Most of your clients' value is locked inside text — tickets, contracts, reviews, emails, knowledge bases — and they want it out. This module is the leader's map of turning text into something a machine can act on: the classic task menu (classification, extraction, sentiment, summarisation, translation), how text becomes numbers, and the one idea that underpins modern search and RAG — embeddings. The era when you built a bespoke NLP pipeline per task is largely over; an LLM does most of it out of the box. But knowing the framing, and knowing the one primitive (embeddings) that LLMs did not make obsolete, is what lets you scope these missions, price them, and decide when a small dedicated model still wins. The recurring question returns in a new form: buy the language commodity, build only the part that's yours.

items.html: Name the classic NLP tasks and frame any text request as one of them — even when an LLM will ultimately solve it
Explain in one breath how text becomes numbers, and why embeddings replaced keyword matching for meaning
Describe what an embedding is well enough to justify semantic search, clustering, dedup, and RAG to a client
Decide "reach for an LLM" vs "reach for a small dedicated model" on cost, latency, privacy, and determinism — and defend it
Scope and price an embeddings/NLP mission (semantic search, routing, feedback analysis, entity extraction)
Recognise what the LLM era made obsolete and what is still worth building yourself

blocks.html: Almost every text request a client brings you is one of a handful of classic tasks wearing a business costume. Recognising the task is half the scoping — it tells you what "good" means, what data you need, and whether a small model or an LLM is the right tool. The tasks haven't changed; what's changed is that one general model now solves most of them zero-shot.

TaskWhat it doesTypical business useSmall model vs LLM lean
Text classificationAssign a label to a documentTicket routing, spam, content moderation, intent detectionSmall at high volume; LLM to prototype or for nuanced/rare labels
Named-entity recognition (NER)Pull structured spans out of text (names, dates, amounts, IDs)Contract/invoice extraction, CV parsing, PII detectionLLM for messy/varied text; small when the schema is fixed and volume is huge
Sentiment / intentScore tone or detect what the user wantsReview analysis, support triage, churn signalsLLM for nuance (sarcasm, mixed); small for cheap high-volume scoring
Topic modelling / clusteringGroup documents by theme without predefined labels"What are people complaining about?", corpus explorationEmbeddings + clustering — neither a classifier nor an LLM call per doc
SummarisationCompress a long text to its essenceCall notes, document digests, exec briefingsLLM — this is where they shine; small models are weak here
TranslationRender text in another languageLocalisation, multilingual supportBuy an API (commodity); LLM if you need tone/context control

blocks.title: Intuition
blocks.html: The menu is still the right vocabulary even though the kitchen changed. When a client says "we want AI to read our contracts," your job is to decompose it: that's NER (pull the parties, dates, amounts) plus maybe classification (contract type) plus maybe summarisation (a plain-language abstract). Each sub-task has a different bar for "good" and a different cost curve. The leader who can't name the tasks ends up scoping one giant vague "AI reads contracts" deliverable — and missing the deadline on the 20% of clauses that actually mattered.

Two tasks deserve a flag. Summarisation and free-form generation have no single right answer, so you cannot score them with accuracy/F1 — evaluation is its own discipline that lives in Large Language Models in Practice. And topic modelling is the odd one out: it has no labels at all, which is exactly what embeddings unlock (concept 3).

subsections.title: From Text to Numbers: Tokens, Keywords, and the Jump to Meaning

blocks.html:
A model cannot consume a string; it consumes numbers. Two steps get you there.

Tokenisation is the chopping step: splitting text into the small units a model actually reads. Modern models don't split on whole words (the vocabulary would be infinite — every typo and proper noun is a new word) but on sub-word pieces, so "tokenisation" might become token + isation. You don't need the algorithm. You need two consequences: (a) cost and limits are counted in tokens, not words (roughly ¾ of a word each in English, far more per character in languages like Japanese or for code) — this is how LLM bills and context windows work, see Large Language Models in Practice; and (b) tokenisation is why models handle never-before-seen words gracefully instead of choking.

The representation question — keyword vs meaning. Once you have tokens, how do you represent a document so a machine can compare two of them?

  • Bag-of-words (the precursor). The simplest representation: a document is just the multiset of words it contains, counts only, order discarded — "the cat sat" and "sat the cat" look identical. Crude, but the foundation TF-IDF refines.
  • Keyword / TF-IDF (the classic). Represent a document by which words it contains, weighting rare-but-informative words ("mitochondria") above ubiquitous ones ("the"). This is how classic search engines worked, and it is genuinely good for matching exact terms. Its fatal flaw for meaning: it has no idea that "car" and "automobile" are the same thing, or that "great" and "fantastic" are close. Different words = different dimensions = zero similarity, even when the meaning is identical. Synonyms split the signal; paraphrases miss entirely.
  • Embeddings (the modern primitive). Represent a document by its meaning as a list of numbers, learned so that texts meaning similar things land near each other — regardless of the exact words used. This is the jump that unlocks everything in the next concept.

The TF-IDF formula is one every practitioner should recognise — it is still a strong, near-free baseline for keyword weighting and classic search:

tfidf(t,d)=tf(t,d)⋅idf(t)whereidf(t)=log⁡Ndf(t)\text{tfidf}(t,d) = \text{tf}(t,d)\cdot\text{idf}(t) \qquad \text{where}\qquad \text{idf}(t)=\log\frac{N}{\text{df}(t)}tfidf(t,d)=tf(t,d)⋅idf(t)whereidf(t)=logdf(t)N​

• tf(t, d) — term frequency: how often term t appears in document d (a word used a lot in this doc matters more for this doc).
  • df(t) — document frequency: how many documents contain term t at all.
  • N — the total number of documents in the corpus.
  • idf(t) — inverse document frequency: rare-across-the-corpus terms score high, ubiquitous ones ("the", "and") score near zero.

A word scores high in a document when it is frequent here but rare everywhere else — exactly the words that characterise the document. Use case: keyword weighting, classic/full-text search ranking, and a cheap, surprisingly strong classification baseline before you reach for anything heavier.

blocks.title: why IDF uses a log
blocks.html: Without the log, a word appearing in 1 of 1M docs would outweigh one in 10 docs by a factor of 100,000 — absurdly spiky. The log compresses that into diminishing returns: going from common to rare boosts the weight, but each further halving of df adds less and less. It dampens the influence of very common terms smoothly instead of letting raw frequency dominate. You don't derive it — you recognise it as "rarity, but with the volume turned down."

BM25 is the length-normalised successor to TF-IDF that powers modern keyword and hybrid search engines — same instinct (rare terms matter, frequent-here terms matter) but with saturating term frequency and a correction so long documents don't win by sheer length. It is the keyword half of hybrid retrieval; for the full formula see RAG & Applied LLM Systems (AI Scientist Path), and for how it blends with embeddings see RAG & Knowledge Systems.

blocks.title: Intuition
blocks.html: Keyword search asks "do these two texts share the same words?" Embeddings ask "do these two texts mean the same thing?" A user searching "how do I cancel my plan" should find a help article titled "Terminating your subscription" — they share zero important keywords. TF-IDF can't bridge that; embeddings were built to. (You don't choose one forever — strong production search often blends both, BM25 for exact terms and embeddings for meaning. See RAG & Knowledge Systems.)

Comparing strings, not meanings — Levenshtein / edit distance. Sometimes the question isn't "do these mean the same?" but "are these two strings nearly the same characters?" The Levenshtein (edit) distance between two strings is the minimum number of single-character insertions, deletions, or substitutions needed to turn one into the other. cat → cot (substitute a→o) → coat (insert a) is two edits, so the distance is 2. Small distance = likely a typo, variant, or duplicate. Use cases: fuzzy matching, deduplication / entity resolution ("Jon Smith" vs "John Smith"), spell-check, and fuzzy joins across messy datasets where keys don't match exactly.

blocks.title: Intuition
blocks.html: Edit distance measures surface similarity (spelling), not meaning — it's blind to synonyms but excellent at catching the same string typed slightly differently. It's the cheap, deterministic first pass for dedup and matching before you ever reach for embeddings.

This is the one idea to take from this entire module. Everything modern — semantic search, RAG, clustering, dedup, recommendation — is built on it.

What an embedding is. An embedding is a piece of text (a word, a sentence, a whole document) mapped to a fixed-length list of numbers — a vector, typically a few hundred to a couple of thousand of them. The model that produces it (an embedding model) is trained so that texts close in meaning produce vectors close together, and unrelated texts produce vectors far apart. That single property — close = similar in meaning — is what makes the numbers useful. The vector itself is uninterpretable (no human reads "dimension 412"); what matters is the distances between vectors.

The embedding space. Picture every possible text as a point in a vast multi-dimensional space. The embedding model is the map that places them. In a good map, "Paris", "London" and "Tokyo" cluster in one neighbourhood (cities); "happy", "joyful", "delighted" cluster in another (positive affect); a furious customer review and a calm one sit far apart even if they share words like "product" and "delivery." You can't draw 1,000 dimensions, but the intuition is exactly the 2-D one: meaning becomes geometry, and geometry is something a computer can search, sort, and group.

Measuring closeness — cosine similarity. To ask "how similar are these two texts?" you measure how close their two vectors point in that space. The standard measure is cosine similarity: it looks at the angle between two vectors, not their length. Same direction → similarity near 1 (very similar); perpendicular → near 0 (unrelated); opposite → near −1. The formula every practitioner should recognise:
cos⁡(θ)=A⋅B∥A∥ ∥B∥\cos(\theta)=\frac{A\cdot B}{\lVert A\rVert\,\lVert B\rVert}cos(θ)=∥A∥∥B∥A⋅B​

where A and B are the two embedding vectors, A · B is their dot product, and ‖A‖, ‖B‖ are their lengths (magnitudes). Dividing by the two lengths is the key move: it cancels magnitude out entirely, leaving only direction. That is why cosine measures meaning (which way the arrow points) rather than length (how long the text is) — a one-line tweet and a long essay on the same topic still score as similar.

blocks.title: Intuition
blocks.html: Think of each text as an arrow from the origin. Two arrows pointing the same way mean "these say the same kind of thing," whatever their magnitude. We use the angle rather than straight-line distance precisely so that a short tweet and a long paragraph about the same topic still count as similar — length shouldn't dilute meaning. In practice "find similar text" reduces to "find the arrows pointing closest to mine," and at scale that search is delegated to a vector database (see Tools, and RAG & Knowledge Systems).
Why this one primitive unlocks so much. Once text is a point in a meaning-space and you can measure closeness, a whole family of problems collapses into the same move — embed, then compare:

CapabilityThe moveConcrete example
Semantic searchEmbed the query, return the nearest documents"cancel my plan" finds "terminate subscription"
Clustering / topic discoveryEmbed all docs, group the nearby onesAuto-discover the 8 themes in 50k reviews — no labels needed
Deduplication / near-dupFlag pairs whose vectors are very closeSpot the same complaint filed five ways; merge duplicate tickets
RecommendationRecommend items whose vectors are near things the user liked"More articles like this one"
RAG (retrieval)Embed a question, fetch the nearest knowledge chunks, feed them to an LLMGrounded answers over the client's own documents → RAG & Knowledge Systems
Classification (cheap)Embed once, train a tiny classifier on the vectorsHigh-volume routing without an LLM call per item

blocks.title: semantic search over a support knowledge base
blocks.html: A SaaS client has 4,000 help articles and a search bar their users hate, because it only matches exact keywords: searching "I was charged twice" returns nothing, since the relevant article is titled "Resolving duplicate billing." You run each article through an embedding model once (a one-off batch job — a few cents to a few euros total) and store the 4,000 vectors in a vector database. At query time you embed the user's phrase and return the articles whose vectors are nearest. "I was charged twice" now lands on "Resolving duplicate billing" because their meanings are close, despite sharing no keywords. No LLM is involved, no model is trained, and the running cost is a tiny embedding call plus a vector lookup per search — milliseconds, fractions of a cent. This is the cheapest, highest-trust win in the whole NLP toolbox, and it is the literal first half of every RAG system. (The second half — handing those retrieved articles to an LLM to write the answer — is RAG & Knowledge Systems.)

The commercial point: embeddings are a commodity you rent, not something you build. Embedding models are cheap, fast, and excellent off the shelf. The value you add is what you embed (the client's proprietary corpus) and the workflow around it — never the maths.

subsections.title: When a Small Model Beats an LLM

blocks.html: The reflex in 2026 is "just call an LLM." For prototypes and low volume, that reflex is right — it's the fastest path to a working demo. But at production scale, a small fine-tuned or classic model routinely wins on the four axes a client actually pays attention to. This is the most important judgement in the module.

AxisWhy a dedicated/classic model can winConcrete bite
Cost at scaleA purpose-built classifier costs a tiny fraction of an LLM call, per itemClassifying 10M tickets/month: an LLM API can run into five or six figures monthly; a fine-tuned small model runs on modest hardware for a fraction
LatencyA small model answers in milliseconds; an LLM round-trip is hundreds of ms to secondsIn-the-loop routing or real-time moderation can't wait on a slow generative call
Privacy / residencyA self-hosted small model keeps data in your perimeterHealth/finance/public-sector data that legally can't leave the building → ties to Data Governance, Privacy & Pseudonymisation
DeterminismA classifier gives the same label every time; an LLM can drift or phrase things differentlyAuditable, repeatable decisions (compliance routing) want a fixed, testable output

blocks.title: Intuition
blocks.html: An LLM is a brilliant, expensive generalist consultant; a small fine-tuned model is a cheap, fast, utterly reliable specialist clerk. You hire the consultant to figure out the problem and handle the weird cases; once the task is well-defined and runs millions of times, you train the clerk to do that one thing. A common, mature pattern is to use the LLM to label a few thousand examples, then train a small model on those labels — you get the LLM's quality baked into a model that's 100× cheaper and faster to run. The LLM designs the job; the small model does the shift work.
A clean rule of thumb for the room:

Reach for an LLM when…Reach for a dedicated / small model when…
The task is fuzzy, varied, or open-endedThe task is narrow and well-defined
You need it working this week (zero-shot, no training data)You have (or can cheaply generate) labelled examples
Volume is low-to-mediumVolume is high and per-call cost compounds
Output is generative (summaries, rewrites, answers)Output is a fixed label, score, or span
Occasional variability is acceptableYou need determinism, auditability, or sub-100ms latency
Data can leave your perimeterStrict privacy / residency forbids external calls

The escalation mirrors the whole path: prototype with the LLM to prove value fast, then graduate the high-volume, well-defined slice to a cheap dedicated model once the spec is stable. Don't fine-tune on day one; don't run a frontier LLM on 50M items on day 100. (LLM economics in full: Large Language Models in Practice.)

subsections.title: The Shift from Bespoke Pipelines to LLMs — What Changed, What Survived

blocks.html:
For a decade, an NLP project meant assembling a bespoke pipeline per task: collect and label thousands of examples, engineer features, train a model, evaluate, maintain it. Each new task (a new entity type, a new language, a new label) was a new project. The LLM era flattened most of that.

What the LLM era made largely obsolete:

  • Per-task data labelling and training as the default first step. An LLM does classification, extraction, and sentiment zero-shot — no training set, working in an afternoon. The prototype that used to take a quarter takes a day.
  • Hand-built linguistic pipelines (rule-based parsers, hand-tuned feature engineering for text, bespoke per-language models). The general model absorbed most of it.
  • The cold-start problem. You no longer need labelled data to begin; you need it only to optimise a high-volume task later.

What survived — and is still worth building yourself:

  • Embeddings + vector search. The LLM did not replace this; modern systems lean on it harder than ever (it is the retrieval half of RAG). This is the durable primitive of the whole module.
  • Small dedicated models for the high-volume, well-defined, latency- or privacy-critical slice (concept 4). The economics didn't go away — they got more important as volume grew.
  • Your proprietary data and domain workflow. The model is a rented commodity; the defensible value is the client's corpus, the integration, the human-in-the-loop process, and the trust layer around it. That is exactly where to spend the build budget.
  • Evaluation discipline. "It demoed well" is not "it works at scale" — knowing whether the text system is actually good is unchanged in importance (see Model Evaluation, Validation & Experimentation).

blocks.title: Intuition
blocks.html: The LLM era didn't delete NLP — it moved the build line. The commodity boundary slid upward: training a sentiment classifier from scratch used to be differentiating work; now it's a one-line API call. Your build budget should chase the new line — proprietary data, retrieval over it, the cheap specialist models that make scale affordable, and the workflow trust — not the parts a foundation model now gives away for free.

These are the four NLP/embeddings missions you can name, scope, and sell. Each is concrete, has a named deliverable, and a real buyer.

  • Semantic search over a document corpus — Problem: "our people can't find anything in our wiki/contracts/knowledge base; keyword search is useless." Deliverable: an embedding-based search service over the client's corpus (ingestion + chunking + embedding + vector store + a search endpoint or UI), with a relevance evaluation on real queries. Scope: 3-6 weeks. Who buys: COO/knowledge-management lead, support leadership, legal ops. Note: this is the natural on-ramp to a RAG mission — scope it as a standalone win first, then upsell RAG & Knowledge Systems.
  • Document classification / routing at scale — Problem: thousands of incoming tickets/emails/documents manually triaged. Deliverable: an automated classifier (LLM to bootstrap labels, then a cheap small model for production volume) that routes each item to the right queue/owner, with a confidence threshold and a human-review path for low-confidence cases. Scope: 4-8 weeks. Who buys: head of support/operations, shared-services lead. Note: sell the cost-per-item-at-scale story explicitly — the LLM-to-small-model graduation is the margin.
  • Customer-feedback / review analysis (themes + sentiment) — Problem: "we have 50k reviews/survey responses/support transcripts and no idea what they're telling us." Deliverable: a themes-and-sentiment report — embedding-based clustering to surface the recurring themes (no predefined labels), sentiment per theme, trends over time, and the verbatim quotes behind each — delivered as a dashboard or a one-off study. Scope: 2-5 weeks. Who buys: product, CX, marketing/insights. Note: high perceived value, low technical risk — an excellent door-opener.
  • Entity extraction from unstructured text — Problem: key facts (parties, dates, amounts, clause types, product IDs) are trapped in PDFs/emails/contracts and re-keyed by hand. Deliverable: an extraction pipeline that turns unstructured documents into structured rows against an agreed schema, with an accuracy report per field and a human-validation step for low-confidence extractions. Scope: 4-10 weeks (driven by document variety and the cost of an extraction error). Who buys: finance, legal, procurement, operations. Note: scope to the fields that carry the cost; "extract everything" is unboundable.

Buy-vs-build for the client: buy the embedding model and the LLM (commodities) and rent the vector store; build only the corpus ingestion, the domain schema, the cheap production model, and the human-in-the-loop workflow. What to charge for: a fixed-price discovery + evaluation, then the build; the recurring embedding/LLM run-cost is passed through or folded into a managed-service retainer. What NOT to promise: a specific extraction-accuracy or search-relevance number before you've seen the client's actual documents — commit to a method and a target, measure on their data, then quote. Never promise "it'll read any document perfectly"; the long tail of weird formats is where these missions overrun.
As of 2026 — treat names/prices as perishable; re-check before quoting.

Tool / CategoryUse hereOSS / PaidNote
Hosted embedding APIs (OpenAI, Cohere, Voyage, Google)Turn text into vectors, no infraPaid (per-token, cheap)Easiest start; compare on quality, price, dimensions, data terms
Open embedding models (e.g. via Sentence-Transformers; leaderboards like MTEB)Self-hosted embeddings, privacy/cost controlOSSUse MTEB to choose; self-host when data can't leave the perimeter
Vector databases (Pinecone, Weaviate, Qdrant, Milvus, pgvector)Store & nearest-neighbour-search vectors at scaleMixed (pgvector OSS)pgvector if you already run Postgres; managed if scale/ops matter
spaCyFast, production NER / classification / tokenisationOSSThe classic-NLP workhorse; great cheap specialist
Hugging Face (models + Transformers + datasets)Pre-trained small models, fine-tuning, hostingOSS + paidThe hub for dedicated/small models and fine-tuning
Hosted LLM APIs (Anthropic Claude, OpenAI, Google, Mistral)Zero-shot classify/extract/summarise; bootstrap labelsPaid (per-token)Prototype fast; watch per-item cost at scale → Large Language Models in Practice
Cloud translation APIs (Google, DeepL, AWS)Commodity translationPaidBuy it; don't build translation
Managed search platforms (Elastic, OpenSearch, Vespa, Algolia)Hybrid keyword + vector search in one engineMixedWhen you need keyword and semantic together

blocks.title: Note on freshness
blocks.html: Embedding model quality and price move fast and the rankings reshuffle quarterly — check a current leaderboard (e.g. MTEB) and live pricing before committing a client to a model.

SituationEmbeddings/NLP is overkill — do this instead
Users search for exact codes, SKUs, IDs, namesPlain keyword/full-text search; embeddings add cost, not value
A handful of fixed categories with obvious keywordsWrite keyword rules / regex; ship in a day
You need to read text a few times a dayLet a person read it; automate the data-gathering around them
The corpus is tiny (a few dozen docs)Read them; or simple search — clustering needs volume to mean anything
"Summarise this once" for one userA single LLM call, or a person — no pipeline
Structured data is already in a databaseQuery it with SQL/BI; don't NLP your way to data you already have

The senior move is to ask "is the meaning actually fuzzy, or are people just searching badly?" A surprising number of "we need AI search" requests are solved by fixing synonyms and filters in the existing keyword engine.

• Jurafsky & Martin — Speech and Language Processing (free draft online) — the field's standard reference; read for framing, skip the heavy maths.
  • Tunstall, von Werra & Wolf — Natural Language Processing with Transformers — practical, Hugging-Face-centric. (Paid)

• Hugging Face NLP / LLM course — hands-on, modern, free.
  • Vendor embedding & RAG guides (OpenAI, Cohere, Anthropic) and the MTEB embedding leaderboard — for current model choice. Date everything.

• Deeper math (embeddings, tokenisation, sequence models, the distributional hypothesis) → Natural Language Processing (AI Scientist Path).
  • Deeper code (building NLP/LLM pipelines, fine-tuning, serving) → NLP & LLMs (ML Engineer Path).

subsections.title: Decision Checklist — scoping an NLP / embeddings mission

items.html: Which classic task is this really? (classification / NER / sentiment / topic / summarisation / translation)
Is the problem meaning (→ embeddings) or exact terms (→ keyword search)?
What volume per month — and does that push us from LLM toward a small model?
Latency, privacy, determinism: any hard constraint that rules out an external LLM call?
Do we have (or can we cheaply generate, via an LLM) labelled examples for the high-volume path?
How will we measure "good" on the client's own data — and against what baseline?
Which fields/labels actually carry the business cost? (Scope to those, not "everything.")

blocks.html:
• "Just throw it all at an LLM" — for millions of items/month, that's a runaway bill and a latency problem.
  • "Build us a custom NER model from scratch" — usually an LLM prototype gets you 80% by Friday; start there.
  • "Semantic search" requested when users actually search for exact IDs/codes (keyword search is the real fix).
  • No labelled data, no plan to get any, and a demand for a high-accuracy guarantee up front.
  • "Summarisation accuracy must be 95%" — there's no single right summary; that metric doesn't exist (see Large Language Models in Practice).
  • A corpus of 30 documents and a request to "discover the themes" — too little to cluster meaningfully.

• "When someone searches today and fails, what did they type and what did they expect to find?"
  • "Of everything you want extracted, which three fields, if wrong, actually cost you money or time?"
  • "How many of these documents/messages arrive per day, and how fast must each be handled?"
  • "Can this text legally leave your systems and be sent to an external API?"
  • "What do you do with the output — route it, report it, decide on it — and who acts on it?"
  • "How will we both agree it's working well enough to go live?"
Before moving on, you should be able to:

items.html: Decompose a vague "AI reads our text" request into named classic NLP tasks
Explain embeddings — close = similar in meaning — and why they unlock semantic search, clustering, dedup, and RAG, without writing a formula
Walk through a semantic-search build end to end and state its (tiny) running cost
Route a task to LLM vs small/dedicated model on cost, latency, privacy, and determinism, with a one-line justification
Scope and price at least two of the four mission archetypes, naming the deliverable and the buyer
Say what the LLM era made obsolete and what's still worth building yourself — and where the build budget should go
